title: The Single Spring
description: From Hooke's Law to harmonic motion

blocks.title: Setup: One Mass, One Spring
blocks.content_html:
Drag the mass and release it. Watch it oscillate.

A mass m is attached to a spring, fixed to a wall. The mass sits on a frictionless surface and can slide left and right. When you pull it and let go, the spring pulls it back. When it overshoots, the spring pushes it the other way. With no friction, it oscillates forever.

The spring exerts a restoring force proportional to how far you stretch it:

$$F = -kx$$

This is Hooke's Law. The constant $$k$$ is the spring constant, measured in N/m (newtons per meter). What does that mean physically? If $$k = 15$$ N/m, then stretching the spring by 1 meter requires a force of 15 newtons. A larger $$k$$ means the spring is stiffer — it takes more force to stretch it the same distance, and it pulls back harder.

The minus sign means the force always opposes the displacement — pull right, force pushes left.

$$x$$ is the displacement from the equilibrium (rest) position. When $$x = 0$$, the spring is relaxed and exerts no force.

Notice in the animation: the mass moves fastest through the center (where the spring is relaxed) and slowest at the extremes (where the spring force is strongest, decelerating it). The force doesn't make it go faster — it reverses it. This is the key to oscillation.

blocks.title: The Equation of Motion
blocks.content_html:
Newton's second law says the net force on the mass equals mass times acceleration:

$$F = ma = m\ddot{x}$$

About those two dots. The notation $$\ddot{x}$$ is Newton's dot notation for time derivatives. Newton invented it in the 1600s and physicists still use it because it's compact when time is the independent variable. The rules are:

  • One dot, $$\dot{x}$$, means the first derivative of $$x$$ with respect to time. That's the velocity — how fast position is changing.
  • Two dots, $$\ddot{x}$$, means the second derivative of $$x$$ with respect to time. That's the acceleration — how fast velocity is changing.
  • You'd add a third dot for the third derivative, and so on, but you almost never need to.

So $$\ddot{x}$$ means exactly the same thing as $$\frac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$$. The dot form is just shorter, and the dot specifically signals "derivative with respect to time" — never with respect to anything else. When you see $$\ddot{x}$$, read it in your head as "x double-dot" or "the acceleration of x."

Now, the only force acting on our mass is the spring force, so we combine Hooke's law with Newton's second law:

$$m\ddot{x} = -kx$$

This is a second-order ordinary differential equation (ODE) — "second-order" because the highest derivative that appears is the second derivative ($$\ddot{x}$$). It says: the acceleration of the mass is proportional to its displacement, but in the opposite direction.

Our job now is to solve this equation — find a function $$x(t)$$ that satisfies it.

blocks.title: Derive the Solution
blocks.prompt: Starting from the equation of motion, derive the solution x(t) and identify the angular frequency ω.

steps.instruction: Divide both sides by m to isolate the acceleration term.
steps.explanation: We divide every term by $$m$$: the left side $$m\ddot{x} \div m = \ddot{x}$$. The right side $$-kx \div m = -\frac{kx}{m}$$. Now, $$-\frac{kx}{m}$$ and $$-\frac{k}{m} \cdot x$$ are the exact same expression — we're just grouping it to separate the constant $$(-k/m)$$ from the unknown function $$(x)$$. Why? Because our next move is to ask: what function, when differentiated twice, gives back itself times a constant? Writing it as (constant) × x makes that pattern visible.

steps.instruction: Assume a trial solution of the form x(t) = Ae^{iωt}.
steps.explanation: We guess that the solution is an exponential. Why? Because exponentials are eigenfunctions of the derivative operator — differentiating an exponential gives back the same exponential (times a constant). This is the standard technique for linear ODEs with constant coefficients.

steps.instruction: Compute the second derivative of the trial solution.
steps.explanation: Taking the first derivative: $$\dot{x} = i\omega A e^{i\omega t}$$. Taking the second: $$\ddot{x} = (i\omega)^2 A e^{i\omega t} = -\omega^2 A e^{i\omega t}$$. Since $$x = Ae^{i\omega t}$$, we can write $$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x$$.

steps.instruction: Substitute the second derivative into the equation of motion.
steps.explanation: We replace $$\ddot{x}$$ with $$-\omega^2 x$$ in the equation $$\ddot{x} = -\frac{k}{m}x$$.

steps.instruction: Cancel x from both sides (x ≠ 0) and solve for ω.
steps.explanation: Since $$x(t)$$ is not identically zero (the mass is oscillating), we can divide both sides by $$x$$. The negative signs cancel too.

steps.instruction: Take the square root to find ω.
steps.explanation: The angular frequency $$\omega$$ (in rad/s) depends only on the spring constant and the mass. A stiffer spring or a lighter mass means faster oscillations. The real-valued solution is $$x(t) = A\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$, where $$A$$ is the amplitude and $$\phi$$ is the phase.

hints: What kind of function gives back a negative of itself when differentiated twice?
Try assuming a solution of the form e^{iωt} — exponentials are the natural guess for constant-coefficient ODEs.

blocks.title: Derive ω = √(k/m) by Hand
blocks.prompt: Work the whole derivation on the canvas — your stylus or finger, your order, your handwriting. Start from m·ẍ = -kx and end at ω = √(k/m). After you pause for a moment, the system reads each line and shows a dot: green if the line matches a valid form in the chain, gray if not. Wandering and dead ends are fine — only the dots that go green count toward finishing. Click "I'm done" once one of your lines reaches the target.
blocks.context_note: When you're ready, you can substitute $$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x$$ — that came from differentiating the trial solution $$x = A e^{i\omega t}$$ in the previous block.
hints: What kind of function gives back a negative of itself when differentiated twice? Cosine and sine — that's where ẍ = -ω²x comes from.
After you substitute ẍ = -ω²x into the equation of motion, both sides have a factor of -x. Cancel it.
Once you have ω² = k/m, take the (positive) square root to get ω.

blocks.title: Guided Version (Fallback)
blocks.prompt: If the freeform canvas above felt overwhelming, work through the same derivation here in guided steps. Each step tells you what to do; you draw the result on the pad and the system checks it before moving to the next.
blocks.context_note: Use the substitution $$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x$$ when the time comes — that came from differentiating the trial solution $$x = A e^{i\omega t}$$ in the previous block.

steps.hint: Same operation on both sides. The $$m$$ on the left cancels.

steps.instruction: Substitute $$\ddot{x} = -\omega^2 x$$ into the left-hand side.
Cancel $$x$$ from both sides and the negative sign too.
steps.hint: Both sides have a factor of $$-x$$. Divide it out.

steps.instruction: Take the square root to solve for $$\omega$$.
steps.hint: Only the positive root has physical meaning here.

blocks.title: Estimate ω
blocks.prompt: Estimate this in your head — no calculator. For k = 15 N/m and m = 0.3 kg, what is ω? Hint: 15 / 0.3 = 50, and √50 is between √49 = 7 and √64 = 8.

blocks.title: Implement and Verify
blocks.prompt: Define k and m, compute ω, create a time array, compute x(t), and verify numerically that the second derivative of x(t) equals -(k/m)·x(t) at every time step using finite differences.
blocks.starter_code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Parameters
k = 15.0   # N/m
m = 0.3    # kg
A = 1.0    # amplitude (m)

# Compute angular frequency
omega = # YOUR CODE HERE

# Time array: 0 to 5 seconds, 1000 points
t = np.linspace(0, 5, 1000)

# Position: x(t) = A * cos(omega * t)
x = # YOUR CODE HERE

# Verify: compute x''(t) numerically using finite differences
dt = t[1] - t[0]
x_ddot_numerical = np.diff(x, 2) / dt**2

# Compute x''(t) analytically: should equal -(k/m)*x
x_ddot_analytical = -(k/m) * x[1:-1]

# Check they match
max_error = np.max(np.abs(x_ddot_numerical - x_ddot_analytical))
print(f"omega = {omega:.4f} rad/s")
print(f"Max error between numerical and analytical x'': {max_error:.6f}")

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
plt.plot(t, x)
plt.xlabel('Time (s)')
plt.ylabel('x (m)')
plt.title('Simple Harmonic Motion: x(t) = A cos(ωt)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
blocks.solution_code:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Parameters
k = 15.0   # N/m
m = 0.3    # kg
A = 1.0    # amplitude (m)

# Compute angular frequency
omega = np.sqrt(k / m)

# Time array: 0 to 5 seconds, 1000 points
t = np.linspace(0, 5, 1000)

# Position: x(t) = A * cos(omega * t)
x = A * np.cos(omega * t)

# Verify: compute x''(t) numerically using finite differences
dt = t[1] - t[0]
x_ddot_numerical = np.diff(x, 2) / dt**2

# Compute x''(t) analytically: should equal -(k/m)*x
x_ddot_analytical = -(k/m) * x[1:-1]

# Check they match
max_error = np.max(np.abs(x_ddot_numerical - x_ddot_analytical))
print(f"omega = {omega:.4f} rad/s")
print(f"Max error between numerical and analytical x'': {max_error:.6f}")

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 4))
plt.plot(t, x)
plt.xlabel('Time (s)')
plt.ylabel('x (m)')
plt.title('Simple Harmonic Motion: x(t) = A cos(ωt)')
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

checks.message: Numerical and analytical second derivatives should match (finite diff error is expected to be small but nonzero)

blocks.title: Practice: Compute ω
blocks.template: For k = {k} N/m and m = {m} kg, what is ω in rad/s?

blocks.title: Preview: The Matrix Formulation
blocks.content_html:
Before we move on, let's rewrite our equation in a form that will generalize to multiple masses and springs.

Define the state vector:

$$\mathbf{z} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ v \end{pmatrix}$$

where $$v = \dot{x}$$ is the velocity. Then the equation of motion becomes a first-order system:

$$\dot{\mathbf{z}} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -k/m & 0 \end{pmatrix} \mathbf{z}$$

This is just our same equation $$\ddot{x} = -(k/m)x$$, rewritten as two first-order equations:

  • $$\dot{x} = v$$ (velocity is the derivative of position)
  • $$\dot{v} = -(k/m)x$$ (acceleration is the derivative of velocity)

Why bother? Because in Lesson 2, we'll have two masses and three springs. That gives us a 4×4 matrix, and finding $$\omega$$ becomes an eigenvalue problem. The matrix formulation scales — the single spring is just the 2×2 warm-up.

blocks.title: Why Stiffer Means Faster
blocks.content_html:
You just derived $$\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$$. Before answering the conceptual check, let's connect that formula back to physical intuition — why does the math come out this way?

Look at the equation of motion at any instant:

$$\ddot{x} = -\frac{k}{m}\,x$$

The left side is acceleration. The right side says: at any displacement $$x$$, the acceleration is $$k/m$$ times that displacement (with a minus sign, pulling back toward zero). Bigger $$k/m$$ means bigger acceleration at every point in the cycle.

Now imagine pulling the mass to the same starting position $$x_0 = 0.1$$ m using two different springs:

  • Soft spring, $$k = 10$$ N/m: restoring force is $$F = -kx_0 = -1$$ N. With $$m = 0.3$$ kg, the acceleration is $$a = F/m \approx -3.3$$ m/s².
  • Stiff spring, $$k = 100$$ N/m: restoring force is $$F = -kx_0 = -10$$ N. Same mass, so the acceleration is $$a \approx -33$$ m/s².

The stiff spring yanks the mass back ten times harder. The mass accelerates ten times faster toward equilibrium, races through the center, overshoots, gets yanked back ten times harder again, and so on. Stiffer spring → larger acceleration at every position → cycles complete faster → higher angular frequency.

The mass $$m$$ works the opposite way. A heavier mass accelerates more slowly under the same force ($$a = F/m$$), so each cycle takes longer. That's why $$m$$ sits in the denominator under the square root — more mass, smaller $$\omega$$, slower oscillation.

And the square root? It's there because $$\omega^2$$ — not $$\omega$$ itself — is what comes out of the equation of motion. Doubling $$k$$ doesn't double the frequency; it multiplies it by $$\sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$. To actually double the frequency, you need to quadruple the spring constant.

This is the physical picture behind $$\omega = \sqrt{k/m}$$. The math just made it precise.

blocks.title: Conceptual Check
blocks.prompt: Why does a stiffer spring (larger k) produce faster oscillations?

options.text: Greater restoring force accelerates the mass more at any given displacement, so it completes cycles faster.
The spring stores more energy, which makes the mass move faster.
options.misconception: Energy affects amplitude, not frequency. A stiffer spring with the same displacement stores more energy, but ω depends on k/m, not energy.

options.text: Stiffer springs are lighter, reducing the effective mass of the system.
options.misconception: Spring stiffness and spring mass are independent properties. In our model, the spring is massless.

options.text: The spring constant changes the wavelength of the oscillation.
options.misconception: Wavelength is a spatial concept for traveling waves. A single mass on a spring oscillates in time only — it has a frequency and period, not a wavelength.
